data.title: Babcock & Wilcox falls amid short report from Wolfpack Research
data.description:
• Babcock & Wilcox (BW) dropped 12% amid a short report from Wolfpack Research. 
  • Wolfpack Research disclosed that it's short shares of Babcock & Wilcox (BW),
  • Babcock & Wilcox didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.

data.title: At least 20 LNG carriers stuck in Persian Gulf as demand soars - WSJ
data.description:
At least 20 liquefied natural gas carriers - roughly half of the available global fleet available for charter - are trapped in the Persian Gulf, as demand from Asia surges, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday.

"The situation is dire and will have a lasting impact on the market, regardless of how quickly the conflict ends," GasLog COO Kostas Karathanos told WSJ; his company operates 34 gas carriers.

Ship brokers said the 20 ships trapped in the Persian Gulf comprise nearly half of all LNG ships currently available for charter, with rates rising to more than $200K/day from less than $98K/day before the start of the Iran war; the total global fleet is ~800 ships.

Energy traders expect LNG prices to rise by early next week, adding to this week’s 40% rise in Asia and Europe. “The effect on LNG shipping will outlast the conflict for a few months,” Karathanos said.

Potentially relevant tickers include (TNK), (FRO), (NAT), (TEN), (STNG), (FLNG), (TRMD), (ECO), (LPG), (ASC), (INSW), (HAFN), (DHT), (CMBT), (LNG), (VG), (FLEX), (GLNG), (NEXT), (NFE)

ETFs: (BWET), (BOAT)

data.title: Viatris resolves case over use of woman’s cancer cells to advance drug research
data.description:
Viatris (VTRS) has reached a settlement with the estate of Henrietta Lacks to resolve allegations that the generic drugmaker used Lacks’ cancer cells without her permission to advance its drug research.

The U.S. company and the estate informed a Maryland federal court on Wednesday that they would resolve the lawsuit with prejudice, meaning cases over similar claims cannot be refiled.

According to Reuters, a lawyer for the Lacks’ estate said that her family was "pleased the parties were able ​to find a way to resolve this matter,” adding that details of the settlement remained confidential.

Lacks’ estate has inked similar settlements with major healthcare companies such as Novartis (NVS) to resolve allegations that the cervical cells of the Maryland woman were used without her consent or understanding to advance biomedical research.

The HeLa cell line, cultivated using Lacks' cervical cancer cells, extracted by a group of doctors at Johns Hopkins Hospital in 1951 before her death, became a cornerstone in biopharma research, as it represented the first human cells to grow and reproduce in lab conditions.

Lacks’ estate had filed a lawsuit against Viatris (VTRS) in 2021, accusing the drugmaker of taking advantage of her cells to study its herpes drug Denavir and depression therapy Mylan-Mirtazapine.

data.title: Mortgage rates rise W/W but buyers are responding to lower rates
data.description:
This week, mortgage rates increased, but buyers are responding to the overall rate decline, according to the latest Freddie Mac (FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.11% as of March 12, up from 6.00% last week and down from 6.65% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.50%, compared to 5.43% a week ago and 5.80% a year ago.

"Despite the modest uptick, buyers are responding to rates in this range, with existing-home sales increasing 1.7% in February," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

"Purchase applications also increased this week, a welcome sign as buyers enter spring homebuying season with rates down more than half a percentage point compared to the same time last year," added Khater.

data.title: Musk expects Digital Optimus ready for use in six months
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk said he expects Digital Optimus, a joint project between Tesla and xAI (X.AI), will be ready for users to experience in six months, according to a post he made on X Thursday morning.

The partnership on the project is part of the agreement regarding Tesla's planned $2B investment in xAI earlier this year.

"Macrohard or Digital Optimus is a joint xAI-Tesla project, coming as part of Tesla's investment agreement with xAI," Musk said.

"You can think of it as Digital Optimus AI being System 1 (instinctive part of the mind) and Grok being System 2 (thinking part of the mind)," he added. "This will run very competitively on the super low cost Tesla AI4 ($650) paired with relatively frugal use of the much more expensive xAI Nvidia hardware. And it will be the only real-time smart AI system. This is a big deal."

Grok is xAI's large language model.

"In principle, it is capable of emulating the function of entire companies," Musk said. "That is why the program is called MACROHARD, a funny reference to Microsoft. No other company can yet do this."

Musk initially revealed the Macrohard project in August 2025.

xAI recently merged with Musk's SpaceX (SPACE), giving the combined company a valuation of $1.25T.

data.title: KLX Energy Services outlines flat to slightly higher 2026 revenue outlook while emphasizing cost discipline and gas basin focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KLX Energy Services Holdings, Inc. (KLXE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chris Baker, President and CEO, opened by highlighting 2025 as "another solid year for KLX despite a choppy market," emphasizing that "the fourth quarter delivered our strongest profitability of the year with adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margin both at 2025 highs." He noted continued optimization of the corporate cost structure, targeted investments in product lines, and a strategic shift toward gas-weighted asset allocation.
  • Baker stated the Northeast Mid-Con segment was the standout, with "dry gas revenue in this segment increased 5.3% quarter-over-quarter and 44% when you compare Q4 of 2025 versus Q4 of 2024, with broad-based gains across most of the product service lines we operate in this segment."
  • In contrast, the Rockies and Southwest faced challenges due to severe weather and reduced oil-directed activity, but the Southwest "margins expanded as we optimized our product and service mix."
  • Baker underlined the company's focus on "higher-margin, technically differentiated work, lean into cost discipline and are very intentional and diligent about where we strategically deploy capital and people."
  • Geoffrey Stanford, Interim CFO, stated, "we generated revenues of approximately $157 million, which was in line with our Q4 guidance. As expected, revenues decreased due to seasonality and budget exhaustion. We generated approximately $23 million of adjusted EBITDA, our highest quarterly adjusted EBITDA of the year and an adjusted EBITDA margin of about 14%, also the high for 2025."
  • Stanford highlighted strong cash flow generation, commenting, "cash provided by operating activities at $13 million, slightly lower than the $14 million in Q3 due to the aforementioned seasonality and budget exhaustion affecting the bottom line. Unlevered free cash flow was $15 million, a 43% increase over Q3."

Outlook

  • Baker described the 2026 outlook as "constructive but measured," projecting "the first quarter to be the low point for the year, reflecting the familiar seasonal combination of customer budget resets, slower restarts of completion programs and weather-related disruptions."
  • He indicated that beyond Q1, "we see a path to a gradually improving market led by gas-directed basins where we believe incremental rigs are more likely to show up before we see a more meaningful recovery in certain oil-directed markets."
  • Baker outlined the company's 2026 revenue framework as "broadly flat to slightly up versus 2025, with the majority of improvement weighted towards the second half of the year, yielding results that trend towards the stronger run rate we delivered in the second half of 2025."
  • For Q1 2026, management forecasted "revenue of $145 million to $150 million, down approximately 3% from Q1 of 2025 despite rig count being down 8% over the same period," with Q2 2026 revenue expected to "rebound to the $160 million to $170 million range, which is higher than Q2 of 2025."
  • Gross capital expenditures for 2026 are expected to be approximately $40 million, with net CapEx in the range of $30 million to $35 million, "with the vast majority of that devoted to maintenance CapEx."

Financial Results

  • KLX Energy Services reported revenues of approximately $157 million for Q4 2025, with adjusted EBITDA of about $23 million and an adjusted EBITDA margin around 14%.
  • Northeast Mid-Con revenue was $69.6 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25.3%, and $15.1 million of total adjusted EBITDA.
  • Rockies revenue declined to $46.3 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $6.9 million; Southwest revenue was $50.9 million, with adjusted EBITDA up to $6.8 million.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA loss improved to approximately $6.3 million in Q4 from $6.6 million in Q3.
  • Cash flow from operations was $13 million, and total debt at year-end was $258.3 million.

Q&A

  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, LLC, asked: "Two positive surprises, very similar to what you reported in 3Q in that at least compared to our estimates, Northeast Mid-Con was stronger and your margins were much stronger than we were modeling. Can you provide...a little bit more color, particularly on the strength in Northeast Mid-Con, which normally would expect to see some hit late in the year because of weather?" Baker responded that "rig count increased about 6% across that entire segment quarter-over-quarter...all of the service lines held up exceptionally well."
  • Ferazani inquired further about margin improvement. Baker replied, "it's really lack of white space, really absorption of fixed costs. sustainably busy and product line mix."
  • Ferazani asked about Southwest revenue and CapEx plans. Baker stated, "we did see some budget exhaustion and completion programs tailing off...we're targeting gross capital spending of $40 million. That's down from $49 million on a year-over-year basis in a year where we think revenue is flat to up."
  • Ferazani questioned the PIK option and covenant relief. Stanford responded, "the waiver -- we...entered into it more as a proactive measure, give us some cushion for the future periods. It goes out 5 quarters or 15 months, so we feel really good about that."
  • Ferazani asked about potential Middle East conflict impacts. Baker explained, "we haven't really seen any reaction to $100 crude yet. And we think most operators are taking a wait-and-see approach."
  • John Daniel (via Ken Dennard) asked about simul frac and coil tubing trends. Baker detailed the company's exposure and competitive positioning, noting "on a stage count basis, if you think about our forecast for this year, we're probably somewhere between 25% and 30% simul-frac, and that's up year-over-year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on segment performance, margin expansion, capital discipline, and risk management; their questions centered on sustainability and drivers of recent outperformance.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident, using phrases like "we focus on higher-margin, technically differentiated work," and "we are approaching the year with a constructive but measured outlook." In the Q&A, management maintained composure and provided detailed, data-driven responses.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a similar tone, with management remaining confident and analysts pressing for details and forward-looking risks but expressing some positive recognition for cost improvements and segment strength.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management signaled slightly lower first-quarter revenue expectations but a broadly flat to slightly up year for 2026, compared to the prior quarter's anticipation of a mid-single-digit revenue decline into Q4 and optimism for a rebound in gas basins.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward gas-weighted asset allocation and cost discipline, while guidance language became more measured regarding the timing and magnitude of a market recovery.
  • Key metrics shifted from Q3’s $167 million revenue and $21 million adjusted EBITDA to Q4’s $157 million revenue and $23 million adjusted EBITDA, with improved margins and continued cost efficiencies.
  • Analysts continued to focus on segment performance, market share gains, and capital flexibility, with repeated questions on sustainability of margin gains and risk management.
  • Management’s confidence in operating leverage and cost structure improvements remained evident, while analysts maintained a focus on potential headwinds and capital allocation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted macro headwinds including seasonality, customer budget resets, and weather-related disruptions, particularly in Q1.
  • There was explicit acknowledgment of the uncertainty tied to Middle East conflict impacts and the potential for commodity price volatility.
  • The company proactively amended debt covenants to provide "adequate cushion for the next 5 quarters" and excluded capital lease balances from leverage ratio calculations during this period, citing "incremental flexibility to fund CapEx, M&A and other capital needs."
  • Analysts voiced concerns about working capital seasonality, cash flow, and the need for covenant relief.

Final Takeaway

KLX Energy Services closed 2025 with its highest quarterly profitability of the year, driven by strong gas basin performance, tight cost control, and strategic asset allocation. Management projects a flat to slightly up revenue outlook for 2026, with the strongest results expected in the second half, and is maintaining disciplined capital spending. Management has proactively adjusted its balance sheet and debt covenants to ensure flexibility amid market uncertainty, positioning the company to capitalize on a market recovery, particularly in gas-weighted basins, while remaining vigilant to macro risks and operational headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sunrise Realty Trust signals $0.30 dividend and expanded credit facility while navigating asset resolution in Q4 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sunrise Realty Trust, Inc. (SUNS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Leonard Tannenbaum, Executive Chairman, highlighted that "as we finished 2025 and turn to 2026, we remain focused on providing loans to sponsors of transitional real estate business plans, primarily in the Southern United States." He noted the portfolio's continued focus on residential loans, mainly senior secured and floating rate, with transaction volume improving in the second half as the Federal Reserve's rate easing cycle began.
  • Tannenbaum stated that during fiscal year 2025, SUNS committed $247 million and funded $224 million in loans, and received $52 million of repayments. As of February 27, the company had committed $62 million to new loans in 2026.
  • Tannenbaum explained, "for the quarter ending December 31, 2025, SUNS generated distributable earnings of $0.27 per share per basic weighted average share of common stock" and discussed the impact of a foreclosed loan on the Thompson Hotel in San Antonio, which reduced distributable earnings by approximately $0.03 per share.
  • Tannenbaum announced, "the Board of Directors has declared a $0.30 dividend per share for the quarter ended March 31, 2026." He also revealed the revolving credit facility increased to $165 million with Customers Bank's $25 million commitment.
  • Brian Sedrish, CEO, described the lending market bifurcation and said, "our approach is differentiated. We focus on originating commercial mortgage loans for sponsors executing transitional business plans, situations that demand a more structured bespoke solution." He detailed Q4 commitments including $26 million for projects in Florida and $30 million for a Houston retail property, and outlined post-year-end activity, notably a $48 million B note refinancing for Hilton Hotels properties.
  • Brandon Hetzel, CFO, reported, "for the quarter ended December 31, 2025, we generated net interest income of $5.2 million and distributable earnings of $3.5 million or $0.27 per basic weighted average common share and had GAAP net income of $1.6 million or $0.12 per basic weighted average common share."

Outlook

  • Tannenbaum emphasized a continued focus on paying a dividend aligned with earnings power, stating, "we remain focused on paying a dividend that is consistent with the earnings power of the business over the medium term."
  • Sedrish expressed confidence in the opportunity set ahead, stating, "I remain confident in the opportunity set ahead and look forward to capitalizing on the attractive opportunities currently in front of us."
  • The declared $0.30 dividend per share for Q1 2026 signals management's expectation of earnings coverage over the next 6 to 12 months, with Tannenbaum noting, "the Board looking forward, felt comfortable that we would get this covered over the course of the next 6 to 12 months in aggregate."

Financial Results

  • Hetzel stated, "we ended the fourth quarter of 2025 with $420.7 million of current commitments and $305.5 million of principal outstanding spread across 16 loans."
  • As of February 27, 2026, the portfolio (excluding the Thompson Hotel) consisted of $442.1 million in commitments and $337 million of principal outstanding, with a weighted average portfolio yield to maturity of approximately 12%.
  • Hetzel disclosed a CECL reserve of $2.1 million or 68 basis points for loans held at carrying value. Total assets were $310.2 million; total shareholder equity was $182 million, with a book value of $13.56 per share.
  • The revolving credit facility, now at $165 million, remains expandable to $200 million and carries an interest rate at 2.75% over SOFR with a 2.63% floor.

Q&A

  • Timothy D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities: Asked about the $62 million committed in February and how market volatility is affecting opportunities. Sedrish responded, "the volatility in the market has created some ups and downs... That created a bit more gap and an opportunity for us to see more of the types of transitional deals that we're focused on... this volatility creates opportunities for us."
  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners: Questioned the drop in the loan pipeline to $652 million from $1.7 billion and asset-specific issues. Sedrish explained, "there has been a differentiation in terms of pricing on the multifamily and industrial side. And we just have been more discerning... $650 million is still in our mind, a really strong pipeline." On the San Antonio asset, Sedrish detailed, "the asset has taken longer to ramp up... sponsor's ability to continue to put dollars in became harder and harder... We do have a series of personal guarantees, which we intend to pursue."
  • Mehta also asked about the dividend's alignment with earnings. Tannenbaum clarified, "the goal is not to overpay our dividend... the Board looking forward, felt comfortable that we would get this covered over the course of the next 6 to 12 months."
  • Tyler Batory, Oppenheimer: Asked about the San Antonio asset's resolution and credit facility growth. Tannenbaum described the foreclosure as "a clean foreclosure, was no delay to it... our intention... is to hire a premier broker as soon as we can." On the credit facility, he noted, "as soon as we resolve what you're going to look for to sort of see re-momentum or additional momentum towards positive earnings... is this asset getting resolved... this really is the only asset that we're concerned about at this time."
  • Batory inquired about capital deployment trends. Sedrish responded, "last year, really reflected the fact that the markets tightened up... the opportunity set was a bit thinner than expected... this volatility does create pretty interesting opportunities for guys like us."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about loan pipeline contraction, asset-specific distress, and dividend coverage, with a slightly negative tone reflected in questions about market volatility, asset foreclosures, and dividend sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks, but showed some defensiveness and caution in the Q&A, especially regarding asset-specific issues, as seen in Tannenbaum's statement, "this really is the only asset that we're concerned about at this time."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from neutral to slightly negative, while management's tone remained steady but more focused on risk management and asset resolution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Distributable earnings decreased from $0.31 per share in Q3 to $0.27 in Q4, with the dividend remaining at $0.30 per share.
  • Q4 saw a higher CECL reserve ($2.1 million vs. $400,000 in Q3) and a decrease in book value per share ($13.56 vs. $13.76).
  • The loan pipeline contracted significantly from $1.7 billion to $652 million, reflecting a more selective approach amid market volatility.
  • Management's tone shifted from emphasizing growth opportunities to addressing risk, asset resolution, and capital deployment discipline.
  • Analysts' questions moved from geographic expansion and financing strategies in Q3 to asset-specific concerns and pipeline contraction in Q4.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward resolving distressed assets and managing capital prudently, while still aiming to capitalize on market dislocation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the Thompson Hotel in San Antonio as the only current asset of concern, citing slow ramp-up, market oversupply, and the sponsor's inability to provide additional capital.
  • Tannenbaum clarified, "this really is the only asset that we're concerned about at this time... we know we have to resolve it quickly."
  • Management intends to pursue personal guarantees and quickly market the asset for sale, while noting that asset resolution is key to restoring earnings momentum and expanding the borrowing base.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about the sustainability of the dividend relative to earnings, loan pipeline contraction, and potential impacts of market volatility on capital deployment.

Final Takeaway

Management remains committed to maintaining stable dividend coverage and capitalizing on transitional lending opportunities, despite headwinds from a foreclosed hotel asset and a reduced loan pipeline. The company's leadership emphasizes swift asset resolution, prudent capital deployment, and confidence in the durability of their portfolio strategy, with continued focus on the Southern U.S. market and structured lending in transitional real estate projects.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Swiss parliament topples proposed bill to limit executive bonuses in financial sector - report
data.description:
Switzerland's lawmakers have reportedly toppled a proposed bill that aimed to limit the bonus-driven incentive system in the country's financial industry.

The bill could have targeted variable pay for senior executives at banks such as UBS Group (UBS) and Raiffeisen (RAIFF) (RAIFY), Bloomberg News reported.

Swiss Parliament's upper house voted against the bill with a 32 to 9 majority.

High bonuses for CEOs are said to have come under fire recently in the country. Swiss politician, who is serving as head of the federal department of finance, Karin Keller-Sutter had reportedly said in 2024 that she would have to work for 30 years to earn the equivalent of UBS CEO Sergio Ermotti's pay.

Today, Keller-Sutter said before the Bern-based parliament that the government favors bonus clawbacks over management failures at large lenders. However, a ban on variable pay is not considered appropriate, according to the March 12 report.

data.title: U.S. stocks sink as war against Iran intensifies, oil prices keep climbing
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Thursday as oil prices continued to climb due to the escalating U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.4%, and the Dow Jones (DJI) was -1.2%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.25%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 6 basis points to 3.72%.

Crude oil (CL1:COM) rose 8.8% to $94 per barrel on Thursday, while Brent (CO1:COM) was also higher at $99.

“I find it hard not to have a central scenario where oil prices will remain very high for a long time, higher than the market current prices,” said Olivier Blanchard, Robert Solow Professor of economics emeritus at MIT.

“There is no reason, whether or not Trump declares that war is over, to think that Iran will not continue for some time to threaten to destroy the ships that try. Why should they stop? The risk will thus remain sufficiently high that most non-Iranian ships will not take the risk. Thus, the shortfall of 20 million barrels a day is likely to last for long,” he added.

The conflict in the Middle East has triggered one of the largest supply shocks of global oil markets in history, leading to disruption of almost 7.5% of the worldwide supply, according to the International Energy Agency.

In economic news, the initial jobless claims barely budged in the past week. The U.S. trade deficit narrowed more than expected in January.

Also, housing starts unexpectedly rose in January, while building permits dropped more than consensus.

The Federal Reserve balance sheet will be released later in the day.

data.title: MariMed outlines 2026 growth strategy as wholesale revenue rises 11% and new state expansions advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MariMed Inc. (MRMD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jon Levine, CEO, highlighted a sixth consecutive year of positive adjusted EBITDA and emphasized the company's 1% revenue growth in 2025, stating "2025 also marked the sixth consecutive year we generated positive adjusted EBITDA." Levine outlined three strategic pillars: capturing meaningful market share in existing markets, expanding brands into new markets, and strengthening the balance sheet. He reported, "Betty's Eddies was the #1 selling edible across the markets where it's available," and described Vibations as ranking fourth in the beverage category. Levine noted expansion efforts into Pennsylvania and New York, and a licensing launch for Betty's Eddies in Maine. The company restructured its Series B preferred shares, extending maturity and enhancing financial flexibility. Levine described retail as "the majority of our revenue and operating cash flow" and announced plans for a new Thrive store in Ohio.
  • Ryan Crandall, Chief Commercial Officer, reported that aggregate wholesale revenue increased 11% in 2025, now representing 44% of total revenue. He stated, "We increased our penetration into dispensaries in 2025 by 200 basis points, selling our brands into 85% of retail stores in the markets in which we operate." Crandall described sequential retail growth of 4% in Q4 and highlighted the success of initiatives such as centralizing retail buying, improving assortments, and expanding the loyalty program, which saw a 31% year-over-year membership increase.
  • Mario Pinho, CFO, reported, "For the fourth quarter, total revenue was $41.7 million, bringing full year 2025 revenue to $159.8 million, representing 1.3% sequential growth in the quarter and 7% growth for the full year." Pinho detailed that retail revenue for the quarter was $23.4 million, up 3.6% sequentially, and wholesale revenue was $17.6 million. He noted, "We ended the year with $8.9 million in cash and cash equivalents, up from $7.3 million at the end of 2024."

Outlook

  • Levine described the primary 2026 growth drivers as continued wholesale penetration, full-year contributions from Delaware's expanding adult-use market, the Maine licensing partnership, and anticipated revenue from the new Ohio dispensary. He added, "We will do everything in our control to move up the time line for distribution of our brands in Pennsylvania and New York."
  • Management indicated that growth strategies do not depend on federal reforms but rather on disciplined capital allocation and execution.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter total revenue was $41.7 million, with full-year revenue at $159.8 million, reflecting 1.3% sequential quarterly growth and 7% full-year growth.
  • Retail revenue for the quarter was $23.4 million, up 3.6% sequentially. Wholesale revenue for the quarter was $17.6 million.
  • Non-GAAP cost of revenue for the quarter was $25 million, with gross profit of $16.5 million and a non-GAAP gross margin of 40%.
  • Total operating expenses for the year were $56.9 million, a 0.7% increase compared to 2024. Operating income for the quarter was $2.4 million and $8.8 million for the year.
  • Non-GAAP EBITDA for the year was $16.9 million, with a margin of 10.5%.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $8.9 million at year-end.

Q&A

  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates, questioned the stabilization of Illinois retail revenues and future deflation. Ryan Crandall responded, "I think we still see some price compression happening in Illinois. But I do tend to agree with you overall that we do believe the market seems to be stabilizing, at least our stores seem to be stabilizing. But price compression is still real in that market, and it is forecasted to compress more in '26."
  • Zuanic inquired about total Illinois and Massachusetts revenues. Mario Pinho replied, "Sequentially in Illinois, we were down and primarily driven by the retail side of our product revenue."
  • Zuanic asked about Delaware's store growth outlook. Crandall answered, "We have seen some new stores getting close to coming online. But at this point, it has been a relatively slow rollout of new stores."
  • Zuanic asked about the Ohio store opening and sustainability of Maryland store growth. Levine stated, "The Ohio store, I can't put an exact time line on it, but we do know that it will be in this year." On Maryland, "that business has been very strong... I do believe that it will continue to see additional growth."
  • Zuanic asked about M&A and asset-light strategy. Levine confirmed, "The big growth opportunities is to still be active in the M&A and we're out there talking to quite a few different groups."
  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital, clarified anticipated contributions from Pennsylvania and New York. Levine responded, "We're hopeful that we can get that operation up and running before the end of the year and build the inventory to start revenue either late the year or early in '27."
  • Gomes asked about additional penetration opportunities. Crandall said, "the ability to go wider and deeper with these customers ... that's our opportunity."
  • Gomes queried interest expense from the refi. Levine answered, "That number is approximately $800,000."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed neutral to slightly positive sentiment, with questions focused on stabilization, expansion timing, and pricing pressure, but also recognizing brand strength and market positioning.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing brand leadership, disciplined strategy, and financial flexibility. Levine stated, "we are confident in MariMed's tremendous upside over the long term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but more focused on operational discipline and capital allocation, while analysts continued to probe on price compression and expansion timelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter's guidance language reflects a continued emphasis on disciplined capital allocation and expansion via both licensing and M&A, similar to the previous quarter, but with greater focus on balance sheet strength and asset-light expansion.
  • Strategic focus shifted more toward wholesale growth, licensing, and leveraging brand leadership in core and new markets, particularly in Delaware, Maine, Pennsylvania, and New York.
  • Analysts maintained a focus on pricing pressure, stabilization in key states, and clarity on expansion timing, echoing previous quarter concerns.
  • Key metric changes included an 11% increase in wholesale revenue and increased retail penetration, with continued margin discipline and cash preservation.
  • Management’s confidence in brand strength and operational execution remained high, as did analysts' cautious inquiries about pricing and market dynamics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing price compression in mature markets, particularly Illinois, and forecasted further compression in 2026.
  • Slow rollout of new stores in Delaware was noted as a challenge, though the company is preparing to meet demand as stores open.
  • Expansion into Pennsylvania and New York is dependent on state approvals and construction timelines, introducing uncertainty in the timing of new revenue streams.
  • The company highlighted a disciplined M&A approach, avoiding deals that could weaken the balance sheet amid industry-wide "fire sales."

Final Takeaway

MariMed closed 2025 with modest revenue growth, strengthened market positions in core states, and expanded into new markets through licensing and managed services. The company’s disciplined approach to capital allocation, margin preservation, and balance sheet restructuring, combined with strong wholesale performance and brand leadership, positions it for continued progress in 2026. Strategic priorities include expanding market share, accelerating brand distribution in new states, and leveraging both organic and M&A-driven growth opportunities, all while navigating persistent pricing pressures and operational challenges in key markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
